name: Binance API
what_it_does: REST and WebSocket API for trading cryptocurrency on the world's largest exchange by volume, covering spot, margin, and futures markets across hundreds of pairs.
use_cases: Agents executing high-volume crypto trades with access to the deepest liquidity globally
Algorithmic trading bots using futures and margin for leveraged strategies
Market data agents streaming order book depth and trade data via WebSocket
Arbitrage bots exploiting price differences across hundreds of trading pairs
DeFi and cross-chain agents using Binance as an on/off-ramp for liquidity
not_for: US-based users who need full regulatory compliance (must use Binance.US, which has reduced features)
Developers who need simple, well-documented APIs — Binance's API surface is enormous and complex
Agents operating in regulated financial institutions that cannot use non-US-regulated venues
best_when: An agent needs the broadest crypto asset selection, deepest liquidity, and lowest trading fees, and is not subject to US regulatory constraints.
avoid_when: You are building for US users or regulated environments — regulatory uncertainty around Binance is significant, and Binance.US has reduced API functionality.

auth.notes: API key plus HMAC-SHA256 or RSA/Ed25519 request signing for all trading endpoints. Public market data endpoints require no auth. API keys have configurable permissions (read, spot trading, futures, withdrawals) and IP allowlist support. Key management is via the web UI.

pricing.free_tier_limits: API access is free; market data endpoints are fully public with no auth required
pricing.paid_tiers: Trading fees: 0.1% spot (base rate); up to 25% discount with BNB token payment; VIP tiers reduce fees further based on 30-day volume; futures fees lower than spot
pricing.notes: No API subscription fee. Fee structure is among the most competitive for crypto trading. BNB token discount incentivizes holding Binance's own token. Binance.US (US version) has somewhat higher fees and fewer trading pairs.

performance.rate_limits: 1200 request weight per minute (each endpoint has a weight); order rate limits separate (100 orders/10 seconds, 200,000/day); WebSocket up to 1024 streams per connection

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST errors return JSON with code (negative integer) and msg fields. Error codes are documented but the list is very long and some codes lack clear descriptions. HTTP status codes used correctly. WebSocket error events carry code and msg.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No built-in client order ID idempotency for all order types. newClientOrderId can be set to track orders, but duplicate submissions may create duplicate orders — agents must implement their own deduplication.
known_agent_gotchas: US regulatory status of Binance (non-US) is uncertain — US-based agents or regulated deployments should use Binance.US or a different exchange
Request weight system is complex — different endpoints consume different 'weight' from the 1200/minute budget; agents must track total weight, not just call count
Timestamps must be within 5 seconds of server time (serverTime endpoint) — clock drift causes signature failures that are hard to debug
newClientOrderId for idempotency is recommended but not enforced — test deduplication behavior carefully before production
API documentation covers spot, margin, futures, and options separately — choosing the wrong API for your use case is easy
WebSocket streams drop after 24 hours and must be renewed — agents require reconnection and re-subscription logic
Binance.US API is different from Binance.com API — code written for one may not work on the other

security_score_components.security_notes: Strong HMAC/RSA/Ed25519 request signing. IP allowlist for API keys. Granular permissions per key. However, Binance has had significant security incidents historically (2019 hack, 2022 BSC bridge exploit) and operates outside Western regulatory frameworks.
